PhD Viva Examination

Non-Destructive Ultrasonic Estimation of Crystal Orientation Fabric Descriptors in Laboratory Ice-Core-Scale Specimens

Jerome Alexander Graves
Doctor of Philosophy · School of Engineering
16 July 2026
Why it matters
Ice records how it flowed, in its crystal fabric.

Crystal-orientation fabric governs how ice sheets deform and how climate is read from ice cores. It is fundamental to ice-core and ice-sheet science.

Jerome A. Graves · PhD viva · London South Bank University · 16 July 2026
The science

Crystal orientation fabric

  • What it is As ice flows and deforms, its crystals rotate so their main axes (c-axes) line up in preferred directions. That shared alignment is the crystal orientation fabric (COF): near-random at the surface, a single maximum under compression, a girdle near ice divides.
  • What bulk COF unlocks Read down a core, the depth profile of fabric type and strength constrains ice flow-law parameters, reveals shear zones, and sharpens projections of ice discharge and sea-level rise. It is the mechanical history of the ice sheet, written in the crystals.
The frontier

Beyond bulk: fabric at the specimen scale, in 3D

  • The limit of bulk COF is a three-dimensional property that varies through the ice. A bulk average, or a single 2-D section, cannot resolve its spatial variability, or how it changes from place to place in the core.
  • What specimen-scale fabric would unlock Resolving fabric at the specimen scale, and ultimately in three dimensions, would tie fabric directly to local deformation, capture the heterogeneity that bulk measurements average away, and let flow models be tested at the scale where the processes happen.
The problem

Specimen-scale, or non-destructive, but not both

  • Methods that resolve fabric at the specimen scale (optical thin sections, EBSD) are destructive, and give only discrete measurements from 2-D cut planes.
  • Non-destructive methods (borehole sonic logging, radar polarimetry) give only a bulk fabric averaged over a large volume, with no specimen-scale resolution.
  • → No established non-destructive, specimen-scale route to fabric orientation without a dedicated facility.
The approach

Ultrasound: non-destructive, but not yet validated at the specimen scale

  • Why ultrasound Non-destructive and directly sensitive to the fabric's elastic anisotropy. X-ray CT and MRI see grain shape, not orientation; neutron diffraction sees orientation but needs a reactor. Ultrasound needs neither: portable and leaving the core intact, it opens the possibility of measuring fabric in the field, on the freshly recovered core, before it is shipped or sectioned.
  • The specimen-scale gap Recent studies (Hellmann et al. 2021; Lutz et al. 2022; Rathmann et al. 2022) validate against bulk fabric: a bulk velocity model, EBSD, or ODF moments. None reports the in-plane orientation error at the specimen scale. That is this thesis's contribution.
Research question

Can ultrasonic timing, measured non-destructively, recover in-plane crystal-fabric orientation in intact ice-core-scale specimens, and where is it reliable?

Objective: develop and evaluate a complete laboratory ultrasonic measurement method for that descriptor, and define its domain of validity, its limitations, and its failure modes.

Model · 1 of 10

Model the physics first

  • The question Before building anything: is there a robust timing observable that carries the in-plane fabric orientation, which geometry recovers it, and how accurately in principle?
  • Interrogated A 2-D finite-difference (FDTD) simulation of the wave through synthetic fabricked ice: candidate reduced forward models across reflection and opposed transmission, over fabric tilt and frequency (1, 2.5, 5 MHz), plus coupling and waveform quality.
  • Found A bias-corrected, gated-envelope time-of-flight carries the expected 2θ azimuthal signal; a reduced path-traced model fits it best; the best-case orientation floor is 1.7-2.2°; and reflection is the tighter, primary pathway. This set the observable, geometries and QC rules carried into the experiment.
Fabricate · 2 of 10

Fabricate the ice specimens

  • Grown to a target fabric The ice is frozen bottom-up with surface agitation (about −5°C), to grow columnar ice aiming for a mostly vertical, single-maximum fabric, a controlled target to test the method against.
  • Two routes, indexed 100 mm disks CNC-machined from that bulk ice (reflection), and cylindrical cores grown in a mould (transmission), each with a durable θ = 0° index mark that later ties the ultrasonic and optical frames.
  • Fabrication QC Screened for cracking, bubbles, frost and geometry before any measurement, separate from waveform QC, so a bad specimen is never mistaken for a bad measurement.
Measure · 3 of 10

Scanner 1: pulse-echo azimuthal (reflection)

  • Build A motorised rotary stage indexes the sample in azimuth; a linear axis advances a broadband contact transducer to the surface; a force sensor catches first contact.
  • How it works At each angle θ the transducer fires a pulse diametrically across the sample; it reflects off the far surface back to the same probe. The round-trip time gives apparent velocity V(θ); halved and fitted as a 2θ response, its extremum is the orientation estimate.
  • Primary pathway A repeatable diametric path, a simple specimen-frame angle (2° steps, ±2.3°), and many specimens for optical comparison make this the primary validation route.
Measure · 4 of 10

The reflection measurements

5
samples
3
frequencies
180
angles / scan
540
waveforms / sample
  • Frequencies Three pulser widths per sample, 0.2, 0.4 and 0.5 µs (nominally ≈2.5, 1.25 and 1.0 MHz), each processed as an independent dataset.
  • Azimuthal scan The full 360° in 2° steps, 180 angles per scan, with 1024 hardware averages at each angle.
  • Observable Apparent velocity V(θ) from the round-trip back-wall echo halved over the 100 mm diameter, fitted per dataset and checked for consistency across the three frequencies.
Measure · 5 of 10

Scanner 2: pitch-catch multistatic (transmission and chord)

  • Build Two contact transducers on opposite sides of an intact cylinder, with motorised specimen rotation plus independent circumferential steering and axial translation of each probe: five axes.
  • Opposed transmission Probes directly opposite (0° / 180°): a single pulse crosses the diameter (single-pass, not halved); rotating the specimen sweeps the direction for an independent angular velocity response.
  • Multi-offset chord Steer the receiver off-opposed and the path becomes an oblique chord; many chords (angles φ1, φ2 and axial offset Δz) give the path diversity for effective-property mapping.
Measure · 6 of 10

The opposed-transmission measurements

1
core, C001
2
depths
90
angles / scan
90
waveforms / depth
  • Geometry Transmitter and receiver directly opposite (0° / 180°); a single pulse crosses the diameter (single-pass, not halved).
  • Scan C001 at 2 transverse depths (6 and 12 mm), 90 angles at 4° over 360°, at 1 MHz with 128 hardware averages: 90 waveforms per depth (180 total).
  • Purpose Apparent velocity V(θ), 2θ fit to the predominant azimuth: the independent check on the reflection result.
Measure · 7 of 10

The multi-offset chord measurements

9
receiver angles
8
depths
12×12
grid / depth
810
max chords / depth
  • Path diversity Steer the receiver off-opposed, ±32° in 8° steps (9 angles), so the path becomes an oblique chord; axial stations at 2 mm (up to 10 mm offset).
  • Coverage Across 8 depths (0 to 14 mm), up to 810 chords per depth (fewer pass QC and enter each inversion).
  • Reconstruction A regularised 12×12 grid per depth, read with coverage and residuals as a feasibility effective-property map, not grain-scale COF.
Quality control · 8 of 10

Quality-control every trace, by pre-declared rules

  • A gate, not a clean-up The acceptance criteria are fixed before the data are seen. A measurement is used only if it passes.
  • Three gates Ultrasonic QC (waveform acceptance, angular coverage and coherence), then optical QC (sampling and image quality), then registration QC (confident frame alignment). Fail any one and the comparison is rejected, or reported descriptively, never as a validation.
Validate · 9 of 10

Validate against a registered optical thin section

  • Ground truth The ultrasonic estimate is checked against a destructive optical thin section, the reference the whole method is measured against.
  • The optical reference machine A crossed-polariser imaging rig rotates the section in 5° steps to read each grain's in-plane extinction azimuth, giving the predominant optical azimuth.
  • Registered A shared θ = 0° index mark ties the optical frame to the ultrasonic scan frame (declared ±3.8°), so both are compared in one coordinate system.
Validate · 10 of 10

The optical measurement

25,992
images / specimen
72
flat-rotation (extinction)
5°
rotation step
12 MP
camera
  • Technique Crossed-polariser microscopy on a 3-axis gimbal (yaw, pitch, roll): the sample is rotated and tilted under a fixed polariser pair, and each grain goes dark (extinction) at its characteristic angle.
  • Images The full rotation grid is sampled, up to 72×19×19 ≈ 26,000 images per specimen, used to segment the grains; the 72 flat-rotation (zero-tilt) yaw frames give each grain's in-plane extinction azimuth, the angle of its intensity minimum.
  • Analysis Grains are segmented, each assigned an in-plane extinction azimuth in [0°, 90°), then pooled into a 2-D orientation tensor for the predominant azimuth θopt and eigenvalues λ1 ≥ λ2.
Key finding 1

Orientation recovery, and its frequency limit

Reflection recovers the in-plane azimuth. At 1.0 MHz (green), four of five specimens fall in the good and moderate bands, the best to 4.3°. Higher frequency (blue) collapses into "poor" as grain scattering dominates, which is exactly what defines the usable regime.

Key finding 2

An independent transmission check

The same fabric-sensitive timing appears in a different geometry. On core C001, opposed transmission recovers the azimuth at both depths, offset from optical by 10.5° and 15.3°, an independent confirmation of the reflection pathway.

Key finding 3

The method knows when it fails

Coarse-grained specimens (B001, B003) start with large errors at high frequency, then improve monotonically as frequency drops, from ~33° into the good band. That signature fingerprints grain-boundary scattering, not fabric weakness, so cross-frequency inconsistency becomes a built-in reliability diagnostic.

Key finding 4 · exploratory

From a single angle to a spatial map

Beyond a single orientation, the exploratory chord pathway maps effective fabric direction through depth (hue is direction, saturation is strength). This is a path-integrated effective property, distinct from the reflection orientation in finding 1. Cell-by-cell against the registered thin section it agrees to a mean of 19.1°, wider than the reflection azimuth, as path integration and volume sampling predict. A feasibility result, not a validated orientation accuracy.

Rigour

Validated, gated, and failure-aware

  • Model-led A physics simulation chose the timing observable, geometry and forward model before any specimen was measured.
  • Pre-declared QC Acceptance criteria were fixed before the data were seen; a measurement counts only if it passes the ultrasonic, optical and registration gates.
  • Registered validation Every estimate is compared against a co-located optical thin section in a shared coordinate frame (declared ±3.8°).
  • Failure-aware The frequency signature of grain scattering is diagnosed, so the method reports where it is, and is not, reliable.
Significance

A fabric probe that leaves the ice intact

  • Fills the gap The first lab-accessible, non-destructive route to in-plane fabric orientation at the specimen scale.
  • Cores survive No cutting, so specimens can be re-measured, kept for mechanical testing, or read in the field on a freshly recovered core.
  • Feeds the science Specimen-scale fabric constrains the flow-law parameters, shear zones and sea-level projections that bulk methods only average.
Originality

What is new

  • Registered specimen-scale validation The first ultrasonic fabric-orientation estimate validated against a co-located thin section at the specimen scale, where prior work validates only against bulk fabric.
  • Failure-mode characterisation A frequency-based diagnostic that separates grain scattering from fabric weakness, so the method self-reports its reliability.
  • A complete measurement chain Model, fabricate, measure, quality-control, validate, delivered end-to-end for non-destructive specimen-scale fabric.
A validated, non-destructive route to in-plane fabric orientation at specimen scale, with its reliability mapped.
Conclusion
